input_candidates.truth_value: To investigate the strengthening mechanisms (dislocation, grain size, ordered phase, solid solution, lattice friction) in Fe-Al based low-density steels with 5-9 wt% Al, with detailed characterization of nano-sized Fe3Al ordered phase and dislocation density evolution, and to deconstruct the yield strength of the Fe-6.8Al alloy into mechanistic contributions.

input_candidates.truth_value: Addition of Al up to ~10 wt% leads to density reduction of up to 8% and formation of ferrite (BCC) phase.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 4 shows SAXS-based diameter and volume fraction of Fe3Al ordered phase as: Fe-5Al (~2.6 nm, 0.4%), Fe-6Al (~2.8 nm, 0.7%), Fe-6.8Al (~2.7 nm, 0.8% as 7 wt% Al in figure table), and Fe-9Al (~3.7 nm, 1.9%); the text quotes a general increase from 2.5 ± 0.15 nm to 3.75 ± 0.2 nm with increasing Al content.

input_candidates.truth_value: Young's modulus reduction upon Al addition was reported by Rana et al., limiting automotive use, but is not measured in this study.
input_candidates.prediction_value: TEM investigation of Fe-6.8Al detected ordered phase size of 2.1 ± 0.2 nm, but the specific annealing condition (5 min or 60 min) of the TEM sample was not explicitly stated in the text; it was mapped to the 5 min sample as the primary studied condition.

input_candidates.truth_value: Yield ratio (YS/UTS) calculated: 98%CR not available, 5 min anneal 0.85, 60 min anneal 0.67.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Dislocation density for the 98% CR sample was reported as ~2.2 × 10^15 m^-2 and for the 750 °C/60 min sample as ~10^12 m^-2; the exact value for the 750 °C/5 min sample was not explicitly stated but was described to show a three-order drop in magnitude upon annealing.

input_candidates.truth_value: Strain hardening rate curves (Fig. 8b) shown for 5 and 60 min annealed samples; higher rate for 60 min.
input_candidates.prediction_value: The yield strength of Fe-6.8Al annealed at 750 °C for 5 min (700 MPa) is significantly higher than the 342 MPa reported by Rana et al. [3] for annealing at 900 °C for 1 min, attributed to the smaller ferrite grain size (25.2 ± 5.0 μm vs 69.2 ± 34.8 μm).

input_candidates.truth_value: The ordered phase identified as Fe3Al with D03 structure; a possible B2 (FeAl) phase was indicated by low-intensity peak at ~23 T in Mössbauer spectra for Al ≥ 6.8%, but volume fraction low.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Engineering stress-strain curves and strain hardening rate curves are shown in Fig. 8, but specific numerical values beyond those in Table 4 were not provided in the text; the strain hardening rate for the 60 min annealed sample was qualitatively described as higher than the 5 min sample initially, with a sudden increase followed by gradual decrease.

input_candidates.truth_value: Lattice frictional stress for Fe-Al at room temperature taken as 41 MPa from literature for 0.4 wt% C steel due to unavailability of data.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Mössbauer spectroscopy indicated that the volume fractions of ordered phase observed by SAXS are lower than those of the DO3 phase observed by Mössbauer spectroscopy because SAXS probes ordered regions with sharper boundaries whereas Mössbauer traces all local environments including diffused boundaries.

input_candidates.truth_value: Comparison with dual-phase Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels: the single-phase Fe-6.8Al after 5 min anneal (YS=700 MPa, elongation 20%) rivals properties of lean alloyed duplex steels (YS=472 MPa, UTS=635 MPa, elongation 19.7% from Rana et al. [8,9]).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Density measurements were undertaken by Archimedes principle as reported in Ref. [15], but no numerical density values were provided in the present text.

input_candidates.truth_value: Shear bands observed at ~40° to RD in 98% CR sample; these indicate non-uniform deformation.
input_candidates.prediction_value: The paper mentions that for very fine ordered domains, solid solution strengthening contribution is difficult to separate from ordered phase strengthening and was therefore ignored in such cases.

candidates.truth_value: Addition of Al up to ~10 wt% leads to density reduction of up to 8% and formation of ferrite (BCC) phase.
candidates.prediction_value: Fig. 4 shows SAXS-based diameter and volume fraction of Fe3Al ordered phase as: Fe-5Al (~2.6 nm, 0.4%), Fe-6Al (~2.8 nm, 0.7%), Fe-6.8Al (~2.7 nm, 0.8% as 7 wt% Al in figure table), and Fe-9Al (~3.7 nm, 1.9%); the text quotes a general increase from 2.5 ± 0.15 nm to 3.75 ± 0.2 nm with increasing Al content.
candidates.truth_normalized: addition of al up to ~10 wt% leads to density reduction of up to 8% and formation of ferrite (bcc) phase.

candidates.truth_value: Young's modulus reduction upon Al addition was reported by Rana et al., limiting automotive use, but is not measured in this study.

candidates.truth_value: Strain hardening rate curves (Fig. 8b) shown for 5 and 60 min annealed samples; higher rate for 60 min.
candidates.prediction_value: The yield strength of Fe-6.8Al annealed at 750 °C for 5 min (700 MPa) is significantly higher than the 342 MPa reported by Rana et al. [3] for annealing at 900 °C for 1 min, attributed to the smaller ferrite grain size (25.2 ± 5.0 μm vs 69.2 ± 34.8 μm).
